The drug encapsulation and loading efficiency were measured to be 86% and 16.6%, respectively. All experiments
were done in triplicate. The FI extract exhibited the highest encapsulation efficiency in PLGA NPs.
The absorption spectra of blank, free FI extract, and FI encapsulated PLGA NPs were observed in the range
of 300–800 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Blank PLGA NPs did not possess any absorption peaks. Free FI extract
spectra showed absorption peaks at 415 nm, 460 nm, 540 nm, 613 nm, and 671 nm, lying in blue, green, and red
regions of the visible spectrum. Whereas FI-PLGA NP’s spectra showed only two peaks of maximum absorption
at 418 nm and 674 nm, other absorption peaks were absent in FI-PLGA NPs. The absence of these absorption
bands indicated entrapment of FI extract into the core of PLGA.
A strong emission spectrum, as illustrated in Fig. 3 (b), was observed at λ = 672 nm and λ = 677 nm when
excited  at  λex = 415  nm  for  FI-PLGA  NPs  and  FI  extract,  respectively.  A  slight  peak  shift  and  decrease  in
fluorescence intensity occurred for FI-PLGA NPs. The blue shift is due to the entrapment of FI extract in the
core of PLGA NPs. Also, this can be related to the hydrophobic environment caused by the PLGA polymer.
Another method to ensure the encapsulation of FI in PLGA NPs is by observing its chemical changes through
FTIR. The spectrum of the PLGA NPs exhibited the characteristic bands at 2937 cm-1, which represent C-H
stretching vibrations (Fig. 4). The characteristic bands at 1745 cm-1 are due to the C = O stretch of the carbonyl
group of -COOH, and C–O ester stretches occurred between 1163 and 1080 cm−138,39.
